name: Colombia
tagline: From the goldsmiths of the Muisca and the legend of El Dorado to a Spanish viceroyalty, the cradle of Bolivar's republic, and a modern nation that fought its way through partisan wars and a long armed conflict toward peace.
summary: Colombia's story begins with the chiefdoms of the northern Andes, above all the Muisca, whose ritual offerings of gold gave rise to the legend of El Dorado, and the megalith carvers of San Agustin and the tomb builders of Tierradentro. Spanish conquistadors led by Gonzalo Jimenez de Quesada founded Santa Fe de Bogota in 1538, and the territory became the heart of the Viceroyalty of New Granada, with the fortified Caribbean port of Cartagena guarding the empire's wealth. Independence was won between 1810 and 1819, crowned by Simon Bolivar's victory at Boyaca, and for a decade Colombia formed part of Bolivar's Gran Colombia before it broke apart in 1830. The nineteenth and twentieth centuries were scarred by Liberal-Conservative wars, the loss of Panama, the bloodletting of La Violencia, and a long internal armed conflict, before the 2016 peace accord with the FARC opened a new and still-contested chapter.

founding.label: Independence from Spain
founding.detail: Colombia traces its birth as a nation to 20 July 1810, when the patriots of Santa Fe de Bogota deposed the Spanish authorities and formed a governing junta in the 'Cry of Independence' (Grito de Independencia), the date still marked as the national day. Full independence was secured by Simon Bolivar's decisive victory at the Battle of Boyaca on 7 August 1819, after which the liberated territory of New Granada joined Bolivar's union of Gran Colombia. When that union dissolved in 1830 the country continued as New Granada and, from the constitution of 1886, took the name it bears today, the Republic of Colombia.

quickFacts.label: Pre-Columbian peoples
quickFacts.value: Muisca, San Agustin and Tierradentro cultures of the northern Andes

quickFacts.label: Capital founded
quickFacts.value: Santa Fe de Bogota, 6 August 1538, by Gonzalo Jimenez de Quesada

quickFacts.label: Independence sealed
quickFacts.value: Battle of Boyaca, 7 August 1819, under Simon Bolivar

quickFacts.label: Panama separated
quickFacts.value: 1903, in the aftermath of the War of a Thousand Days

quickFacts.label: Peace accord
quickFacts.value: 2016 agreement with the FARC; Nobel Peace Prize to President Santos

eras.title: Goldsmiths and Megalith Carvers
eras.standfirst: Long before any Spanish sail reached the Caribbean coast, the highlands and valleys of present-day Colombia were home to chiefdoms whose mastery of gold and stone produced the legend of El Dorado and the necropolises of San Agustin and Tierradentro.
body: The northern Andes of South America were settled for thousands of years before European contact, and by the time of conquest the region was a mosaic of chiefdoms rather than a single empire. The most prominent were the Muisca (or Chibcha), who occupied the high plateau around modern Bogota and Tunja. Organised under paramount chiefs and skilled in farming, salt mining, weaving, and trade, the Muisca were above all renowned for their goldwork, casting and hammering objects whose meaning was spiritual rather than commercial: for them, unworked gold held no value, and metal acquired significance only when transformed into offerings.

Muisca religious practice gave rise to one of the most enduring myths of the Americas. At the sacred lake of Guatavita, a newly installed chief was said to be covered in gold dust and to cast precious objects into the waters as an offering. The Spanish, hearing of a gilded man, coined the phrase el dorado, the golden one, and the search for his fabled kingdom drew expeditions deep into the continent. The exquisite Muisca Raft, a votive gold figure showing the ceremony, survives as the emblem of this tradition and is today a treasure of the Museo del Oro in Bogota.

Other societies left monuments in stone. In the upper Magdalena valley, the culture of San Agustin carved the largest group of religious megaliths and monumental sculptures in South America between roughly the first and eighth centuries. Nearby, the people of Tierradentro hollowed out elaborate underground tombs, or hypogea, painting their walls with geometric designs. Both sites were inscribed as UNESCO World Heritage in 1995, standing as testimony to complex, stable, and artistically sophisticated peoples of the pre-Hispanic northern Andes.

events.title: San Agustin megaliths
events.summary: In the upper Magdalena valley, the San Agustin culture carves the largest assemblage of monumental religious statuary in South America, later a UNESCO World Heritage site.

events.title: Tierradentro tombs
events.summary: The people of Tierradentro hew vast painted underground burial chambers into the mountains, a unique record of a developed pre-Hispanic Andean society.

events.title: Rise of the Muisca
events.summary: The Muisca chiefdoms flourish on the high plateau around Bogota and Tunja, celebrated for goldwork, salt, and trade across the Andes.

events.title: The El Dorado tradition
events.summary: At Lake Guatavita, Muisca ritual offerings of gold to the waters give rise to the legend of El Dorado that would lure Spanish expeditions inland.

pullquote.text: For the Muisca, gold held no worth until it was worked; only as an offering did metal acquire its meaning.
pullquote.attribution: On the goldsmithing tradition of the Muisca

eras.title: Conquest and the Viceroyalty of New Granada
eras.standfirst: Spanish conquistadors overran the Muisca highlands in the 1530s, founded Santa Fe de Bogota, and built a colonial society whose wealth flowed through the fortified Caribbean port of Cartagena and was governed from the Viceroyalty of New Granada.
body: Spanish navigators reached the Caribbean coast at the very end of the fifteenth century, and over the following decades expeditions pushed into the interior in search of gold and the kingdom of El Dorado. The decisive conquest came when Gonzalo Jimenez de Quesada led an arduous march up the Magdalena River and into the highlands, subduing the Muisca and founding the city of Santa Fe de Bogota on 6 August 1538 as capital of the New Kingdom of Granada. The establishment of an audiencia, a royal court of justice and administration, at Bogota in 1549 opened the colonial era in earnest.

Colonial society rested on the labour of Indigenous and, increasingly, enslaved African people, organised through the encomienda and later other forms of forced work in mines, fields, and households. Catholic missions and the church shaped daily life and culture, while the population grew mixed over generations. On the Caribbean, Cartagena de Indias became the chief Spanish port and slave market of the region; repeatedly besieged by pirates and rival powers, it was ringed with the most extensive system of military fortifications in South America, an engineering achievement of the sixteenth to eighteenth centuries later honoured by UNESCO.

To govern this strategic territory, the Spanish crown created the Viceroyalty of New Granada, first established in 1717, briefly suppressed, and permanently restored in 1739 with its capital at Bogota. At its height it embraced present-day Colombia, Panama, Ecuador, and Venezuela. By the late eighteenth century, the strains of distant Bourbon rule, heavy taxation, and the example of revolutions abroad bred discontent; the Comunero revolt of 1781 against new taxes signalled the gathering pressures that would, within a generation, erupt into a struggle for independence.

events.title: Founding of Bogota
events.summary: Gonzalo Jimenez de Quesada founds Santa Fe de Bogota after subduing the Muisca, establishing the capital of the New Kingdom of Granada.

events.title: Audiencia of Bogota
events.summary: The crown establishes a royal court and administrative tribunal at Bogota, opening the formal colonial era in New Granada.

events.title: Cartagena fortified
events.summary: Cartagena de Indias is built into the most complete fortified port in South America, guarding the empire's wealth against repeated pirate and naval assaults.

events.title: Viceroyalty of New Granada
events.summary: Spain governs the territory through the Viceroyalty of New Granada, capital Bogota, encompassing modern Colombia, Panama, Ecuador, and Venezuela.

events.title: The Comunero revolt
events.summary: A popular rising against new Bourbon taxes sweeps the interior, an early sign of the unrest that would lead to the independence movement.

figures.name: Gonzalo Jimenez de Quesada
figures.blurb: Lawyer turned military commander who led the harrowing expedition up the Magdalena into the Muisca highlands and founded Santa Fe de Bogota in 1538, opening the conquest of the territory that became Colombia.

pullquote.text: Cartagena has the most extensive and one of the most complete systems of military fortifications in South America.
pullquote.attribution: UNESCO World Heritage Centre, on the port and fortresses of Cartagena

eras.title: Independence and Gran Colombia
eras.standfirst: A movement that began in Bogota in 1810 became, through years of war, the liberation of New Granada at the Battle of Boyaca in 1819, and the heart of Simon Bolivar's vast republic of Gran Colombia, which broke apart within a decade.
body: The collapse of Spanish royal authority during the Napoleonic occupation of Spain opened the door to independence across the Americas. In Bogota, the events of 20 July 1810, remembered as the cry of independence, brought a patriot junta to power, and similar movements arose across New Granada. The early years of the struggle, however, were chaotic and divided between centralists and federalists, a period later called the Patria Boba, the Foolish Fatherland. Spain reconquered much of the territory in 1815-1816 under a harsh pacification, executing many patriot leaders.

The cause was revived by Simon Bolivar, the Venezuelan-born Liberator, in alliance with the New Granadan general Francisco de Paula Santander. In 1819 Bolivar led an audacious army across the flooded plains and the high, freezing paramo of the Andes to surprise the royalists. After engagements at Pantano de Vargas, his forces won the decisive Battle of Boyaca on 7 August 1819, scattering the Spanish army and opening the road to Bogota. The victory effectively secured the independence of New Granada and made possible the liberation of the wider region.

Bolivar's ambition was a single great state. At the Congress of Angostura in 1819 and the Congress of Cucuta in 1821, the union later known as Gran Colombia was organised, joining New Granada, Venezuela, and Ecuador, with Bolivar as president and Santander as vice president. But the union was strained from the start by vast distances, regional jealousies, and the rivalry between Bolivar's centralism and Santander's federalist legalism. Rebellions, an attempt on Bolivar's life, and the secession of Venezuela and Ecuador broke the state apart; Bolivar died near Santa Marta on 17 December 1830, and Gran Colombia dissolved that year.

events.title: Cry of independence
events.summary: A junta seizes power in Bogota, beginning the New Granadan independence movement amid the crisis of Spanish royal authority.

events.title: Battle of Boyaca
events.summary: Bolivar and Santander rout the royalist army at the Boyaca bridge, securing the liberation of New Granada and opening the road to Bogota.

events.title: Congress of Cucuta
events.summary: The republic of Gran Colombia is formally organised, uniting New Granada, Venezuela, and Ecuador under Bolivar's presidency.

events.title: Breakup of Gran Colombia
events.summary: Venezuela and Ecuador secede and Bolivar dies near Santa Marta; the union dissolves, leaving New Granada as a separate state.

figures.name: Simon Bolivar
figures.role: The Liberator, President of Gran Colombia
figures.blurb: Venezuelan-born revolutionary who led the liberation of New Granada at Boyaca in 1819 and founded Gran Colombia. His dream of a single great republic fractured before his death near Santa Marta in 1830.

figures.name: Francisco de Paula Santander
figures.blurb: New Granadan commander who fought alongside Bolivar at Boyaca and served as vice president of Gran Colombia. A champion of constitutional, federalist government, he became a founding figure of the later Colombian republic, remembered as the Man of Laws.

pullquote.text: After crossing the freezing paramo of the Andes, Bolivar's army shattered the royalists at the bridge of Boyaca.
pullquote.attribution: On the decisive battle of 7 August 1819

eras.title: Partisan Wars and the Loss of Panama
eras.standfirst: The nineteenth-century republic was repeatedly torn by civil wars between Liberals and Conservatives over the church, the constitution, and the structure of the state, culminating in the catastrophic War of a Thousand Days and the secession of Panama in 1903.
body: After the breakup of Gran Colombia, the remaining state passed through several names, New Granada, the Granadine Confederation, and the United States of Colombia, before the Constitution of 1886 created the centralised Republic of Colombia. Politics hardened into two great rival parties: the Conservatives, who favoured a strong central government and a close alliance with the Catholic Church, and the Liberals, who pressed for federalism, secular government, and free trade. Their disputes were not merely parliamentary; through the century they erupted again and again into regional and national civil wars that cost tens of thousands of lives.

The bloodiest of these conflicts was the War of a Thousand Days, fought from 1899 to 1902 between Liberal insurgents and the Conservative government. It was the most destructive civil war in Colombian history, killing perhaps one hundred thousand people, estimates vary widely, and leaving the country exhausted, impoverished, and militarily weakened. The war's devastation set the stage for a national humiliation that followed almost immediately.

The isthmus of Panama, long a province of Colombia, was the chosen route for an interoceanic canal. When the Colombian congress rejected the Hay-Herran Treaty of 1903 with the United States over its financial terms, Panamanian separatists, encouraged and protected by U.S. warships dispatched by President Theodore Roosevelt, declared independence on 3 November 1903. Colombia, its army drained by the recent war, could not respond, and the United States promptly recognised Panama and secured the canal zone. The loss of Panama left a lasting grievance in Colombian national memory.

events.title: United States of Colombia
events.summary: A Liberal-led federal constitution reorganises the country as the loosely united States of Colombia, intensifying partisan and regional rivalry.

events.title: The Constitution of 1886
events.summary: A Conservative-led charter creates a centralised Republic of Colombia with a strong presidency and a renewed alliance with the Catholic Church.

events.title: War of a Thousand Days
events.summary: Liberals and the Conservative government fight Colombia's deadliest civil war, with an estimated 100,000 dead, leaving the country ruined.

events.title: Separation of Panama
events.summary: Backed by U.S. warships, Panama secedes after Colombia rejects the canal treaty; the United States recognises it and secures the canal zone.

pullquote.text: With an estimated one hundred thousand dead, the War of a Thousand Days was the most destructive civil war in the history of Colombia.

eras.title: La Violencia and the National Front
eras.standfirst: The mid-century brought modernisation and the rise of the magic-realist imagination of Gabriel Garcia Marquez, but also the assassination that ignited La Violencia, a decade of partisan slaughter that the two parties ended only by agreeing to share power.
body: Colombia entered the twentieth century as a largely rural, coffee-exporting nation, and the interwar decades brought growing cities, organised labour, and reformist Liberal governments. Yet the old partisan hatreds endured beneath the surface. They burst into the open on 9 April 1948, when the popular Liberal leader Jorge Eliecer Gaitan, a fiery orator who championed the urban poor and the rural campesinos, was assassinated in central Bogota. The killing set off the Bogotazo, an explosion of rioting that gutted the capital and stunned the nation.

Gaitan's murder unleashed La Violencia, a decade of undeclared civil war between Liberal and Conservative partisans that ravaged the countryside through the 1950s. Massacres, reprisals, and the displacement of families turned whole regions into killing grounds; estimates of the dead, necessarily imprecise, commonly range from around two hundred thousand to several hundred thousand. The savagery deepened under Conservative rule and was only partly checked by the brief military government of General Gustavo Rojas Pinilla after 1953.

To end the bloodshed, the leaders of the two parties struck a remarkable bargain. Under the National Front (Frente Nacional), agreed in 1956-1958, the Liberals and Conservatives undertook to alternate the presidency and divide public offices equally between them for sixteen years. The pact halted the partisan war and restored constitutional civilian rule, but by closing politics to all other movements it left no legitimate outlet for new social demands, helping to set the conditions for the armed insurgencies that emerged in the following years.

events.title: Assassination of Gaitan and the Bogotazo
events.summary: The killing of Liberal leader Jorge Eliecer Gaitan triggers the Bogotazo riots that devastate Bogota and ignite a decade of bloodshed.

events.title: La Violencia
events.summary: Partisan warfare between Liberals and Conservatives ravages the countryside, killing an estimated two hundred thousand or more Colombians.

events.title: The National Front
events.summary: Liberals and Conservatives agree to alternate the presidency and share offices, ending partisan war but closing politics to other movements.

events.title: Garcia Marquez and Macondo
events.summary: The journalist and novelist Gabriel Garcia Marquez gives literary form to Colombia's history of violence and wonder, later winning the 1982 Nobel Prize.

figures.name: Jorge Eliecer Gaitan
figures.blurb: Charismatic Liberal politician who electrified the urban poor and rural campesinos with calls for social justice. His assassination in Bogota on 9 April 1948 set off the Bogotazo and the era of La Violencia.

figures.name: Gabriel Garcia Marquez
figures.blurb: Colombian writer whose masterpiece One Hundred Years of Solitude (1967) made the fictional town of Macondo a mirror of Latin American history. He won the Nobel Prize for Literature in 1982 and became the foremost exponent of magic realism.

pullquote.text: Many years later, as he faced the firing squad, Colonel Aureliano Buendia was to remember that distant afternoon when his father took him to discover ice.
pullquote.attribution: Gabriel Garcia Marquez, One Hundred Years of Solitude (1967)

eras.title: Armed Conflict and the Search for Peace
eras.standfirst: From the 1960s Colombia was gripped by a long internal armed conflict among leftist guerrillas, right-wing paramilitaries, the state, and drug-trafficking organisations, a war the 2016 peace accord with the FARC sought to end even as violence and the legacies of the drug trade persisted.
body: In the wake of the National Front, several leftist guerrilla movements took up arms, the largest being the Revolutionary Armed Forces of Colombia (FARC), formed in 1964 from peasant self-defence groups, alongside the National Liberation Army (ELN) and others. From the 1980s the conflict grew enormously more complex and brutal with the rise of powerful drug cartels, centred on cocaine, and of right-wing paramilitary forces that fought the guerrillas while committing massacres of their own. The state, the insurgents, the paramilitaries, and the traffickers all became entangled in a war that drew in millions of civilians.

The human cost was immense and is best understood through careful estimates rather than precise figures. Colombia's official Truth Commission, reporting in 2022, concluded that around 450,000 people were killed in the conflict between 1985 and 2018 and that many tens of thousands more were forcibly disappeared, with some nine million people registered as victims, most of them displaced from their homes. Eight of every ten killed were civilians. The drug war of the 1980s and 1990s, the assassinations, kidnappings, and bombings of the cartel era, and the paramilitary massacres are all part of this sober reckoning.

After repeated failed attempts, the government of President Juan Manuel Santos and the FARC negotiated for four years in Havana and signed a peace agreement in 2016, under which the FARC disarmed and became a political party, and a Special Jurisdiction for Peace was created to judge wartime crimes. Santos was awarded the Nobel Peace Prize that year, although Colombian voters narrowly rejected the first version of the accord in a plebiscite, forcing its revision. Implementation has been uneven, dissident factions and the ELN remain in arms, and in 2022 Colombia elected Gustavo Petro, a former guerrilla, as its first leftist president, on a promise to pursue a wider total peace. The country today remains a vibrant democracy still grappling with violence, inequality, and the long shadow of its conflict.

events.title: Founding of the FARC
events.summary: Peasant self-defence groups consolidate into the Revolutionary Armed Forces of Colombia, the largest of the leftist guerrilla movements.

events.title: Cartels and paramilitaries
events.summary: Cocaine cartels and right-wing paramilitary forces intensify the conflict with assassinations, bombings, kidnappings, and massacres of civilians.

events.title: Peace accord with the FARC
events.summary: After four years of talks in Havana, the Santos government and the FARC sign a peace agreement; Santos receives the Nobel Peace Prize.

events.title: Truth Commission report
events.summary: The official Truth Commission estimates about 450,000 killed in 1985-2018 and millions displaced, the great majority of the dead civilians.

events.title: Election of Gustavo Petro
events.summary: Colombia elects Gustavo Petro, a former guerrilla, as its first leftist president, pledging a policy of total peace with remaining armed groups.

figures.name: Juan Manuel Santos
figures.blurb: President of Colombia from 2010 to 2018 who pursued the negotiations in Havana that produced the 2016 peace agreement with the FARC, for which he was awarded the Nobel Peace Prize.

pullquote.text: Of every ten people killed in the armed conflict, eight were civilians.

figures.name: Simon Bolivar
figures.blurb: Founder of Colombian independence, victor of Boyaca in 1819, and president of the short-lived Gran Colombia, whose dream of a united republic shaped the early history of the nation.

figures.name: Francisco de Paula Santander
figures.blurb: Bolivar's comrade-in-arms at Boyaca and vice president of Gran Colombia, a champion of constitutional government remembered as a founder of the Colombian republic.

figures.name: Gabriel Garcia Marquez
figures.blurb: The author of One Hundred Years of Solitude and Colombia's most celebrated writer, who won the Nobel Prize for Literature in 1982 and made magic realism a window onto his country's history.
